observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor continue to
exhaust all available legal remedies to ensure the full settlement and recovery of
the accountability of the former MT.

With the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation with revisions
that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to:

a. exhaust all means in order to substantiate the amount of P124,017.25
(net) pertaining to three bank accounts with PNB. If proven futile, collate
all previously available documents, reports and correspondences, then
consider requesting for write-off for the purpose of fair presentation in
the FS and not for extinguishment of any accountability thereof;

b. coordinate with the PGR and request the details or any related
transaction documents pertaining to the P58,878.28. Also, initiate an
inquiry with the concerned bank to clarify the credit memos associated
with the unidentified amount of P28,560.00. Henceforth, make extra
effort in coordinating with the concerned depository bank or agency and
request for the appropriate supporting documents thereof to serve as the
basis for recording the book reconciling items in order to present fairly
the balance of CIB-LCCA in the FS; and

c. reconcile the discrepancies noted and adjust the Cashbook and the books
of accounts accordingly, and henceforth ensure that reconciliation is
made carefully and timely.

For the Accounts Receivable that have been outstanding for more than
six months, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require the Acting MT to intensify their collection efforts by regularly sending
demand letters and a payment schedule to the delinquent stall occupants and
business owners pursuant to Section 170 of R.A. No. 7160.

For the long outstanding and dormant receivable accounts, we reiterated
our recommendation with revisions that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Accountant to (a) exert all efforts to verify the nature of the dormant
receivables and determine their existence and validity by sending demand
letters or any communications to the concerned persons or agencies, copy
furnished the Audit Team; and (b) consider the applicability of the guidelines on
the proper disposition of dormant accounts provided in COA Circular No. 2023-
008 for fair presentation of receivable accounts in the FS. This is without
prejudice to the management’s responsibility to continue its efforts to collect
the said receivables.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Assessor to (i) ensure the submission of the Assessment Roll
indicating the RPT collectible for the year to the MT for the updating of the
latter’s records which will be the basis for the setting-up of RPT/SET
receivables at the beginning of the year; and (ii) explain in writing the
error noted in the summation of the total RPT/SET receivable based on
their Schedule and ensure that reports prepared are accurate and
submitted in a timely manner;

b. Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the MT to ensure that
RPT/SET receivables are set up in accordance with Section 20 of the
MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and (ii) coordinate with the Assessor’s
Office on the errors noted and prepare the adjusting entries, as
appropriate; and

c. MT to (i) based on the records submitted by the Municipal Assessor,
update the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index card
and prepare a certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the
amount due and collectible for the year and forward the list to the
Municipal Accountant within the prescribed period; and (ii) continue to
adopt strategies (e.g., information campaign, barangay caravan) to
intensify the collection of RPT and SET and to persuade taxpayers to
take advantage of early payment discounts.

a. Inventory Committee to (i) conduct the complete physical count of all
inventories and semi-expendable properties in accordance with Section 124 of
the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Item 4.7.8 of COA Circular No. 2022-
004 and submit the corresponding RPCI and RPCSP to the Audit Team within
the prescribed period; and (ii) reconcile the result of the physical count with
the property and accounting records to ensure reconciliation of both records
prior to the submission of inventory reports to the Audit Team;

b. Supply Officer to (i) ensure that the Summary of Supplies and Materials
Issued (SSMI) and Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) for all issued supplies and
materials are timely prepared and submitted to the Accounting Office for
proper and timely recording of related expense accounts; and (ii) regularly
update the Stock Cards and ensure that these are reconciled with the Supplies
Ledger Cards;

c. Municipal Accountant to (i) regularly update the Supplies Ledger Cards and
ensure that these are reconciled with the Stock Cards; and (ii) prepare the
necessary adjusting entries upon receipt of the SSMI and RIS to properly
account the issued/distributed inventory items;

d. Inventory Committee and Municipal Accountant to explain in writing the
discrepancies noted between the RCPI and FS for Inventories accounts; and

e. Disposal/Appraisal Committee to facilitate the disposal of the
unserviceable properties pursuant to Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445.

With the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the
Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor (a) together with the
Municipal Accountant to complete and submit to the Audit Team the noted
lacking documents; and (b) register the Deed of Absolute Sale with the Registry
of Deeds for the processing of title in compliance with Section 4(6) of P.D. No.
1445 and Item 13.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We also enjoined the management to process the titling of the land to
protect the rights and ownership of the Municipality. Moreover, ensure that
payment of CGT and DST is in accordance with Section 24 (D) (1) of the NIRC
of 1997, as amended by R.A. No. 10963.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to (a) analyze the nature of the payables under the Due to NGAs
account and return the unexpended balances of the fund transfers that pertain
to projects that have already been completed. Make sure to secure an official
receipt for the refund to be made; and (b) ensure that the nature/purpose of the
fund transfer and the name of the source agency are provided in the Schedule of
Due to NGAs and other liabilities accounts.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal Accountant to draw an adjusting entry to reclassify the
above-noted misclassified account to fairly present the balances of Due to LGUs
and Trust Liabilities accounts in the FS.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together
with the concerned municipal employees, submit a written explanation for the
continuous failure to strictly observe the provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A.
No. 9184 and submit the lacking documents mentioned above.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Mayor
instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that supporting documents for
disbursements are proper and accurate pursuant to Section 4(6) of P.D. No.
1445.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. BAC to submit a written explanation for the failure to strictly observe the
provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and submit the lacking
documents mentioned above; and

b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that supporting documents for
disbursements are complete, proper and accurate pursuant to Section
4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.

For procurements made after the effectivity date (February 25, 2025) of the
IRR of R.A. No. 12009, or the NGPA, the management is reminded to observe
strictly its provisions and its IRR in the procurement of goods to ensure
adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness, efficiency,
proportionality, accountability, public monitoring, procurement
professionalization, and sustainability in government procurement.

With the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the
Municipal Mayor:

a. require the MDRRMO and MPDC to (i) prepare a revised or supplemental
LDRRMFIP and AIP, respectively, for all additional funds appropriated for
DRRM during the year to ensure that the fund will be utilized accordingly;
and (ii) incorporate the list of PPAs charged to the unexpended LDRRMF in
the STF in the LDRRMFIP and AIP;

b. require the Municipal Accountant and Acting MT to ensure that all
unexpended balances of QRF and Mitigation Fund – MOOE, as well as the
savings from capital outlays, are consistently transferred to the STF at year-
end pursuant to Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002; and

c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to be guided by the new accounts
provided in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2024-006 in accounting for
semi-expendable properties.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together
with the concerned municipal employees:

a. closely monitor the implementation of development projects identified in
the AIP and see to it that these projects are promptly and timely
implemented in accordance with the schedule in the APP for the benefit of
the constituents and ensure that the development projects to be funded out
of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-
ready pursuant to Item 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1;

b. consider the reversion or re-appropriation of the savings from prior years’
completed projects to other developmental projects under the 20% DF
pursuant to Section 322 of R.A. No. 7160 and Item 3 of the general policies
of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 96-263 for the optimal utilization of the
said idle funds; and

c. ensure that all PPAs incorporated in the AIP are specific or provided with
sufficient details, otherwise, secure authorization from the local
sanggunian for the implementation of PPAs with generic term
appropriations.

With the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendations with some
revisions that the Municipal Mayor:

a. instruct the GAD Focal Person to adjust/revise the DILG-endorsed GBP
after the receipt of the approved/enacted Annual and Supplemental
Budgets to conform with the latter and inform in writing the concerned
DILG Office of changes in the DILG-endorsed GPB pursuant to Item
6.0.C.7.2 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01;

b. instruct the GFPS to prepare the GAD Accomplishment Report in
accordance with the instructions provided in Annex E of the PCW-DILG-
DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01;

c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly reflect in the List of
Vouchers Charged against the GAD Fund the actual expenditures
charged against the fund; and

d. require the members of the GFPS to formulate the GAD Agenda and
furnish a copy to the Audit Team.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that
the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. HRMO to execute a Job Order Contract indicating the essential elements
of a valid contract in hiring job order personnel;

b. Heads of offices to review the DTRs and ARs of job order personnel
under their supervision prior to approval of same to ensure that the
DTRs are properly prepared and that the specific and actual work
accomplishments are indicated in the individual ARs; and

c. Municipal Accountant to ensure that payments to job order personnel are
supported with complete and proper documentation pursuant to Items
1.1.1 and 1.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We reiterated our recommendation and management agreed that the
Municipal Mayor and Sangguniang Bayan, headed by the Municipal Vice
Mayor, expedite the revision and updating of the LRC to be able to update the
taxes and fees rates pursuant to Section 191 of R.A. No. 7160 to generate funds
for the delivery of essential services.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
conduct reconciliation of depreciation expenses and accumulated depreciation accounts against the depreciation schedule and make revisions to the schedule and/or JEVs, if necessary;
revise the format for the Lapsing Schedule, recompute the depreciation expenses for CY 2024 and accumulated depreciation balances as of January 1, 2024 and 2025; and
identify the net book value of all PPE items replaced by subsequent rehabilitation projects and prepare JEVs to derecognize such in the books.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit to the Audit Team a written justification, including other necessary documents and reports, for further evaluation thereof.
We also recommended and the Municipal Mayor and all employees/officials concerned agreed to implement inventory management controls as required in the Manual on NGAS for LGUs.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
instruct the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accountant a more detailed summary of delinquency real property and special education taxes, showing the basic real property and special education tax receivables per delinquent taxpayer as of December 31, 2024;
instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a JEV to record the total delinquency of real property and special education taxes as of December 31, 2024, following the proposed adjusting journal entry:
instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare detailed subsidiary ledgers for the deferred taxes and Discount on Advance Payments accounts for CY 2024 per taxpayer and per year to which the advance payments and discounts are to be applied, and prepare JEVs to recognize income and discount from advance payments to be applied in CY 2024, following the proposed adjusting journal entries:
enforce internal controls so that the Municipal Treasurer can expedite the updating of all RPTARs, and prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant with a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible as of January 1, 2025. Consider assigning dedicated personnel for the purpose, among other controls; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to implement the modified accrual method of accounting for real property and special education tax transactions starting CY 2025 pursuant to Section 19 of the Manual, starting with the accrual of the receivables as of January 1, 2025.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
require the Municipal Accountant to make an adjusting journal entry to properly record the unliquidated financial assistance to Implementing Agencies using the proposed adjusting journal entry below:
require the Implementing Agencies to submit immediately the liquidation reports of the subject financial assistance totaling ₱485,000.00 and ensure proper recording of expenses based on the Report of Checks Issued and the Report of Disbursements;
submit to the Audit Team the noted lacking documents, including a written explanation on the noted deficiencies, for further evaluation; and
ensure that all fund transfers to Implementing Agencies be executed with a MOA, and require the submission of the Report of Checks Issued and the Report of Disbursements within ten (10) days after the end of each month or the end of the agreed period for the project in accordance with COA Circular No. 94-013 for the proper and timely recording of actual expenses.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
retrieve all debit and credit entries to the intra-agency accounts of all funds and prepare the necessary elimination journal entries;
prepare an elimination working trial balance and post the elimination journal entries to all affected accounts. Ensure that the intra-agency accounts are fully eliminated; and
revise and submit to the Audit Team the CY 2024 financial statements based on the restated amounts, including the elimination working trial balance, for further evaluation.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
submit a written justification to the Audit Team on the above-cited deficiencies for further evaluation;
confer with the PRA to be properly guided with the next courses of action regarding the reclamation project with the end in view of avoiding forfeiture thereof; and
confer with the DENR to be properly guided with the next courses of action regarding the necessary permits to use the tracks of land declared as Forestlands with the end in view of avoiding any penalties thereof.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the LDRRM Officer to expedite the preparation of separate subsidiary ledgers for the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account in accordance with Paragraph 5.1.12 of COA Circular No. 2012-002, in aid of the preparation of the CY 2025 financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
submit a written justification, including other documents or records requested, to the Audit Team on the above-cited deficiencies for further evaluation;
conduct a thorough records/documents tracing, as far as practicable, on the Land account totaling ₱3,616,809.08 with the end view of identifying the specific parcels of land owned by the Municipality and for the preparation and maintenance of subsidiary ledgers; and
expedite the registration of the parcels of land identified to be owned by the Municipality of Alcantara under the Torrens Title System.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
prepare a JEV to recognize the prepaid insurance, following the proposed adjusting journal entry:
prepare and maintain a detailed monitoring of all prepayments, such as for rent, registration, insurance, and all others of similar nature, with the end in view of ensuring that the same be adjusted every year-end to recognize the expired portion as expense in compliance with Section 64 of Volume I of the Manual;
submit a reconciliation on the noted difference of ₱15,486.03 between the total Insurance Expenses per books and per GSIS confirmation; and
confer with the GSIS to conduct a thorough reconciliation of records to the Due to GSIS account and make proper adjustments, if warranted.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
instruct the Municipal Treasurer to determine the detailed breakdown of the total undistributed RPT shares totaling ₱813,015.00 per barangay and immediately issue checks for the release thereof; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain subsidiary ledgers on the Due to LGUs account pertaining to the component barangays’ share of RPT and maintain summary schedules for monitoring thereof.

We recommended that Management conduct repairs for all Water Supply System assets to restore their functionality in anticipation to the incoming waterworks system project of the Provincial Government of Cebu, with the end in view of avoiding eventual wastage of resources from the non-usage of these assets.

We recommended and Management, through the concerned office, agreed to request the substitution of the retention money for each of the infrastructure projects with irrevocable standby letters of credit equivalent to the supposed retention money, provided, that the project was on schedule and was satisfactorily taken; otherwise, demand from each of the contractors for the collection of retention money equivalent to 10 per cent of the total contract cost.

We recommended that Management instruct the LDRRM Officer and confer with the SB, who agreed to immediately craft and approve the revised CY 2025 LDRRMFIP, if necessary, taking into consideration the actual amount of appropriation of the CY 2025 current year LDRRMF and the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF balance as of January 1, 2025, with the end in view of ensuring that the revised LDRRMFIP reflects the aggregate amount of LDRRMF and the related PPAs.

We recommended and Management through the concerned offices agreed to ensure that the identified programs, projects, and activities be implemented in order to utilize the available cash in the Trust Fund maintained in the depository bank to prevent these from becoming dormant. Otherwise, initiate steps to return the funds to the source agencies and close the bank accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Prioritize the approval of the disbursement voucher for the payment of the renewal of fidelity bonds;
instruct the Municipal Treasurer to stop the practice of allowing work immersion students assigned in the MTO to handle cash and collections; and
ensure that all accountable officers whose duties permit or require the possession or custody of government funds be properly bonded.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer, who agreed to immediately initiate the process of disposal of all obsolete accountable forms still in his possession and accountability pursuant to Section 86(H) of the LTOM, 2nd Edition.

We recommended that Management:
a. 	submit a written explanation on the above-noted observations together with its proposed action plans or measures if warranted for evaluation by the Audit Team; and
b. 	establish and adapt written guidelines in the hiring of JO personnel taking into consideration the necessity for each office, qualifications, duties/responsibilities and limitations, pay structure, procedures on hiring, performance evaluation and retention, and absorption to Plantilla position, among others.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
issue an executive order for the formation of Fire Brigades on all municipal-owned buildings and structures that can accommodate at least 50 persons in compliance with Division 2 of the 2019 Revised IRR of RA No. 9514;
ensure that the members of Fire Brigades undergo trainings and practice drills on fire prevention, fire suppression, rescue, emergency medical services and related emergency response in compliance with Divisions 3 and 4 of the 2019 Revised IRR of RA No. 9514; and
coordinate with the BFP to conduct a comprehensive inspection on all municipal-owned buildings, structures and facilities in order to identify non-compliance and potential fire hazards, implement appropriate measures to mitigate them effectively, and ensure compliance with the issuance of appropriate documents, reports and certificates such as FSICs, among others.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
issue an EO assigning a focal person tasked in monitoring of submission of the quarterly and annual SEF utilization reports to the Sangguniang Bayan, the DepEd CO, DBM Regional 7, and DILG Region 7 with the end in view of ensuring compliance with the reporting and submission timelines as required in Section 6.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01 series of 2017, starting CY 2025; and
ensure that the submission of quarterly and annual SEF utilization reports is duly acknowledged by these offices or agencies for proper documentation thereof.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit a written justification to the Audit Team on the above-noted deficiencies and cause the revision of EO No. 13-A, series of 2021 taken into consideration the noted deficiencies in the composition of the GFPS, TWG, Secretariat, and M&E Team with the end in view of achieving full compliance with Section 4.1.A and 5.0 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the:
GAD Focal Person, who agreed to confer with the PCW, the DILG, or other government authorities on the availability of capability trainings such as gender sensitivity training and HGDG, among others, as provided in Item 4.1(A)(3) of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended and ensure that all elected officials and department heads to attend with the end in view of applying such capabilities towards the implementation of the Magna Carta of Women; and
Municipal Budget Officer, who agreed to appropriate sufficient funds for capability-building programs on GAD.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor continue to exhaust all available legal remedies to ensure the complete settlement and recovery of the total accountabilities of Mr. Larry M. Enore, retired Municipal Treasurer, made a settlement totaling P452,235.25, and the accountabilities of three other accountable officers: Larcy Galay, Efren Montaño, and Milda Galin. This may include the filing of case in court, as well as pursuing other administrative or criminal actions against them for committing an offense in the performance of their official duties.
observations.managementAction:
Not Although the Implemented accountabilities of Mr. Efren Montaño, and Ms. Milda Galin were fully settled in CY 2024 while Mr. Larry M. Enore made a settlement totaling P452,235.25, and the accountabilities of three other accountable officers: Larcy Galay, Efren Montaño, and Milda Galin. This may include the filing of case in court, as well as pursuing other administrative or criminal actions against them for committing an offense in the performance of their official duties.
The accountabilities of Larcy Galay and Gerardo Marquez remained uncollected/unsettled because Mr. Gerardo is already deceased and demand letter could not be sent to his heirs as their whereabouts is unknown.
Reiterated/ Revised in Observation Nos. 1 and 3 in Part II of this report

observations.recommendation: We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact and timely all her collections and strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 69(1) of P.D. No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Implemented Collections for CY 2024 were deposited intact and timely.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to remind all the collectors to observe the existing rules and regulations on the proper use of all accountable forms in their custody and its exercise caution in the issuance of the same to avoid frequent cancellations.
observations.managementAction: Implemented Although the cancellation of OR could not be entirely avoided, the Acting Municipal Treasurer was able to minimize its occurrence.

observations.recommendation:
For the Accounts Receivable that has been outstanding for more than six months, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to intensify their collection efforts by regularly sending demand letters and a payment schedule to the delinquent stall occupants and business owners pursuant to Section 170 of R.A. No. 7160.
For the long outstanding and dormant receivable accounts, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert all efforts to verify the nature of the dormant receivables and determine their existence and validity by sending demand letters or any communications to the concerned persons or agencies, copy furnished the Audit Team, otherwise, apply for write-off in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed by COA Circular No. 2023-008. This is without prejudice to the Management's responsibility to continue to exert efforts to collect the said receivables.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the accountabilities of Mr. Gerardo Marquez under Due from Officers and Employees totaling P33,993.12 to Other Receivables account pursuant to Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction:
Implemented Demand letters dated November 25, 2024 were sent to various accountable officers with long outstanding accountabilities. Despite this, dormant and long outstanding receivable accounts still exist in the books of the Municipality due to the absence of records/documents which also hindered the finalization of the request for write-off.
The Municipal Accountant prepared the adjusting journal entry on April 23, 2024 to reclassify the accountabilities.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
The Assistant Municipal Treasurer to (i)submit to the Accounting Office a certified list of taxpayers showing the amount due and collectible as at December 31, 2021and furnish a copy of which to the Audit Team;          (ii) ensure henceforth that the same list is being provided to the Municipal Accountant every year for the setting-up of receivable accounts; and      (iii) adopt strategies (e.g., information campaign) to intensify the collection of real property taxes and to persuade taxpayers to take advantage of early payment discount; and
The Municipal Accountant to draw a JEV and present in the FS the RPT and SET Receivables and its corresponding Deferred RPT and SET Income as at December 31, 2021 and henceforth ensure that the receivable accounts are being established at the beginning of every year.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Together with the LDRRMO and Municipal Budget Officer, (i) provide the revised or supplemental LDRRMFIP and AIP for the year showing the PPAs for the unprogrammed amount of P59,850.00 and for the utilized amount sourced from the special trust fund of P3,771,089.34; and (ii) henceforth ensure that all appropriations have corresponding PPAs and all utilization of special trust fund are included in the LDRRMFIP and are authorized by the Sanggunian through the approval of the AIP;
Together with the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant, transfer the remaining and actual unexpended QRF and Mitigation Fund – MOOE to the special trust fund and draw a journal entry to effect adjustment in the books; and
Together with the LDRRMO, Municipal Budget Officer, and Municipal Accountant, (i) review all documents/reports pertaining to the LDRRMF and explain the noted differences thereof; and (ii) henceforth ensure that those are mathematically accurate and consistent with other cognate reports.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Together with the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, fast track the implementation of the identified PPAs under the DF and henceforth see to it that all PPAs are incorporated in the APP; and
Together with the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, explain and provide basis for treating the savings in 2020 (which became funds actually available in 2021) as continuing appropriations in 2021.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
The GFPS to (i) revisit the figures in the submitted GAR and Project Accomplishment Report and reconcile them with the actual cost; (ii) correct or revise the GAR insofar as the actual cost/expenditure and HGDG PIMME scores are concerned; and (iii) henceforth be circumspect in the preparation of accomplishment reports and its supporting papers;
The GFPS and the heads of the implementing departments to (i) provide supporting documents which were noted to be lacking in the disbursement vouchers for the transactions listed above; and henceforth, (ii) the Municipal Accountant who also does the pre-audit of the municipality must require complete documentation prior to processing of payments; and
The GFPS to accomplish the GAD Agenda and GMEF Organizational Assessment Questionnaire and furnish a copy to the Audit Team.

Thus, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, who also serves as the LSB Co-Chairperson, see to it that the formulation and approval of the SEF Budget is within the schedule set by the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1. Moreover, ensure that the PPAs thereto are implemented timely and accordingly.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal General Services Officer/Municipal Treasurer to maintain a separate record or property card for each category of the property, plant and equipment of the Municipality.  Likewise, conduct annually the inventory count of properties and submit to the Audit Team the result thereof not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 124 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the Inventory Committee to comply with the mandatory procedures in the disposal of all obsolete and unserviceable properties pursuant to the provisions of Sections 379, 380 and 381 of RA 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to determine the sources of these funds and either return the excess to the source agencies or request authority from source agencies to use the funds for other priority projects of the municipality. Further, regular monitoring and analysis should be conducted to ensure that fund transfers are liquidated within the prescribed period depending on their nature and purpose.

We recommended that Management submit proof that these are not splitting of government contracts.  In addition, we recommended that procurement of meals and snacks or catering services for its various activities be done through competitive bidding or any alternative mode, when justified by conditions, following the guidelines prescribed in RA 9184, its IRR and related issuances.

We recommended that the BAC use the direct retail purchase of POL products pursuant to Item 10 Section V of Annex H of the IRR as an alternative mode of procurement instead of shopping. The Direct retail purchase of the required POL products shall be carried out in accordance with pertinent accounting principles and practices as well as of sound management and fiscal administration provided that they do not contravene existing laws and regulations applicable to financial transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned officials to secure proper permit and register the same with the DENR as required in Republic Act No. 9175. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipality participate in the aggressive forest protection program geared towards eliminating illegal logging and other forms of forest destruction by registering the chainsaw to regulate the ownership, possession and use thereof to prevent them from being used in illegal logging or unauthorized clearing of forests.

We recommended that the Management procure the common-use supplies and equipment from the Cebu Regional Depot of PS-DBM in accordance with DBM Circular Letter No. 2011-6 dated August 25, 2011, Administrative Order No. 17 and pertinent provisions of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184. We also recommended that the Management comply with the documentary requirements stated in GPPB Resolution No. 17-2020 for those CSE items which are not available in the PS-DBM at the time of procurement before pursuing the procurement from other sources.

We recommended that the Management proof that the statements of work accomplished were inspected by the Municipality.  Henceforth, comply with the conditions set forth in COA Circular No. 2007-001 for the release of financial assistance to NGOs/POs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to conduct inspection within the defects liability period and prepare an Inspection Report to support the issuance of Final Acceptance which should be one year from the project completion pursuant to the pertinent provisions of R.A. 9184 and COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended that Management intensify/expedite the hiring or promotion of qualified personnel to enhance its services and attain full implementation of the Municipality’s programs, projects and activities for the optimal benefit of the constituents as well as to provide equal opportunities to the deserving individuals in the community.  Likewise, we recommended that Management adhere to the pertinent Civil Service rules and regulations on the hiring of job order workers.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor, MPDC and MBO agreed to submit an electronic copy of the Annual Investment Plan to the Climate Chang Commission pursuant to the directive in the LBM No. 80 and the DBM-CCC-DILG JMC No. 2015-01.

We recommended that Management, through the BAC, submit to the Audit Team valid justifications on the award of contract to suppliers without the FDA’s License to Operate as drug or device retailer/wholesaler/ distributor.

Henceforth, we further recommended that Management strictly comply with the pertinent law, rules and regulations in the procurement of goods/services including the related issuances of the GPPB to ensure that contracts are awarded to eligible suppliers with valid licenses and permits.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide the Audit Team with the list of beneficiaries with their signatures signifying their acceptance/acknowledgement of the goods.    We also recommended that the Management  set up inventory account for the purchased welfare goods for distribution as required under COA Circular No. 2014-002.
We recommended and Management agreed to comply with Section 8 of RA 11313 and Section 9 of its IRR to ensure enforcement of the provisions of the Safe Spaces Act.

observations.managementAction: The management reviewed the items listed that were delivered beyond the terms stated, it was found out that they had missed to correct the pre-filled Purchase Order delivery terms.  Instead of Immediately/Upon Receipt of P.O., it should have been referred to NTP, PhilGeps Posting, or the actual delivery period/contract duration as reflected in the PHilGeps posting or NTP.  The Notice to Proceed takes effect 10 days from receipt.  The delivery period has a time range of 30 days from the time the NTP took effect.  Upon review, all identified procurement were actually delivered within the specified delivery period/contract duration.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct those responsible for the procurement to submit a written justification for the noted over-priced medical supplies/equipment.  We also recommended that Management, henceforth,  be guided by the prices of Essential Emergency Medicines and Medical Devices set by DOH during this COVID-19 Health Crisis to ensure that the government achieve the best value for money in sourcing essential medical goods.
observations.managementAction: The management explained that the medical supplies and equipment was done sometime in May 2020.  During this time, medical supplies and equipment used for COVID-19 were quickly running out due to high demands and the municipality was in dire need to procure the medial supplies and equipment to combat COVID-19.  However, despite the urgent need, the BAC made sure that canvass for the market prevailing price was conducted by the end-user in the municipality and the neighboring towns.  The most advantageous amount that resulted from the canvass was used in the procurement of the medical supplies and equipment.  Furthermore, the LGU was not informed and did not receive a copy of the Price Freeze memo on the procurement of supplies for COVID-19 and in haste was not able to research and review the same prior to the procurement.  Going forward, the management will make sure that before any procurement, appropriate efforts are made to ensure all government issuance relative to each procurement activities are thoroughly reviewed by all end users and BAC.
observations.reason: Attached Certification that unit price indicated in the Purchase Request for supplies and equipment for RHU personnel is based from the Local Market Prevailing Price.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management refrain from using the 20% Development Fund for expenses that do not fall under the guidelines set forth under DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2017-1 on the utilization of 20% Development Fund for the priority programs and projects of the barangay.  Likewise, submit written explanation on the use of 20% Development Fund to pay out expenditures not covered in the said circular.
observations.managementAction: The management explained that the LGU had an existing street lighting system but was already worn out and busted, thus, the need to rehabilitate and install a completely new lightning system.  The LGU then allocated fund from the 20% Development Fund for the installation of the new street lighting system.  The procurement of supplies amounting to ₱90,715.00 was only part of the total project cost to construct and install the new street lighting system.  This project was done under administration and not under straight contract hence the need to procure all supplies as well as labor payroll to complete the said project.  Thus, Management considered all the cost associated with the complete rehabilitation and installation of streetlighting project as capital expenditure and not merely as repairs and maintenance.
observations.reason: The management submitted explanation or justification and received by the Audit Team on March 5, 2021.

The Municipal Mayor direct the LGU GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG) to (i) observe the schedule for GAD Planning and Budgeting as provided in Item 4.1.C.5 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and the amendments in JMC No. 2016-01 to facilitate timely submission to the Local Planning and Development Office and the subsequent review and endorsement by the DILG Provincial Office; and (ii) submit the GAD AR and its supporting documents to the Audit Team and henceforth, always be guided by Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 in the submission of the GPB and GAD AR;
The Municipal Accountant be guided by Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for proper documentation of transactions; and
The Municipal Budget Officer make sure that expenditures charged to each programs, projects and activities do not exceed the available appropriations pursuant to Section 4(1) of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that Management direct the Inventory Committee to conduct the yearly physical count of PPE, render the report thereon and provide a copy of the report to the audit team on or before January 31 in the following year pursuant to Section 266 of the GAM for LGUs.

We recommended that the concerned Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to strategize and ensure the submission of the 2020 year-end financial reports and other related accounting reports on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Property Officer and other officials concerned to explain the failure to monitor the delivery of the furniture and equipment purchased and that liquidated damages be computed, imposed and collected on the payment for the delivery beyond the delivery terms and conditions.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to submit the December 2020 official receipts, disbursement vouchers, supporting documents and other reports on time.

We recommended that Management submit an explanation on what have transpired during the process and make representation to the DSWD as to the proper action on this matter.  Furthermore, Management send demand letters to the ineligible beneficiaries for the refund of the amount received totaling ₱744,000.00 and ensure that these disbursed funds are collected and returned to DSWD.

We recommended that Management direct those responsible for the procurement to submit a written justification for the noted over-priced medical supplies/equipment.  We also recommended that Management, henceforth,  be guided by the prices of Essential Emergency Medicines and Medical Devices set by DOH during this COVID-19 Health Crisis to ensure that the government achieve the best value for money in sourcing essential medical goods.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit explanation/justification on non-compliance with the mandatory posting of the updated APP, NOA, and other information relative to the Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act in the GPPB Online Portal as mandated in GPPB Circular No. 1-2020.

Henceforth, we recommended that the BAC always be updated with the issuances and advisories of the GPPB by visiting their website regularly to avoid non-compliance in the future.

We recommended that the management refrain from using the 20% Development Fund for expenses that do not fall under the guidelines set forth under DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2017-1 on the utilization of 20% Development Fund for the priority programs and projects of the barangay.  Likewise, submit written explanation on the use of 20% Development Fund to pay out expenditures not covered in the said circular.

We recommended that management require the Inventory Committee to submit the RPCI to the concerned auditor as proof of the  yearly physical count of inventory items conducted on or before January 31 of the following year.  Likewise, submit to the Office of the Auditor the RPCI of the other supplies recorded under Special Education Fund.

We recommended that Management require the concerned official to prepare and post the reports as required under Section 4 of DBM LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 2020. We also recommended that the Department Heads must be aware of whatever guidelines intended to them in order to ensure proper implementation thereof.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to submit request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts from the Commission on Audit with supporting reports and other documents in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.

Based on the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation that the
Municipal Mayor:
a. as the Local Chief Executive, should have the political will to strictly
implement its solid waste management plan and remind the Board of
their duties and responsibilities enumerated in Section 12 of R.A. No.
9003;
b. through SWMB, i) ensure effective and efficient enforcement of
environmental ordinances towards the attainment of the objectives of the
LGU’s Ten-Year Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan; ii) strictly
implement and monitor the segregation of wastes at source and collection,
transport and handling of solid waste as required by the provisions of
Rule VIII, IX and X of the IRR of R.A. No. 9003; and iii) adopt necessary
measures to properly manage and monitor the operation of its temporary
open dumpsite to mitigate the possible risks to the health and
environment of the constituents;
c. through the Liga ng mga Barangay, encourage active participation of
barangay officials to support the solid waste management program of the
government by educating their respective constituents on the proper
segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes
and prohibition on open burning of solid waste;
d. recommend to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) policies and possible
measures that will ensure full compliance by the barangays and its
constituents on the provisions of R.A. No. 9003; and
e. through SWMB to fast track the processing of various requirements for
the operation of a sanitary landfill in accordance with Sections 40 and 41
of R.A. No. 9003 the soonest possible time to avert adverse environmental
consequences.

headed by the Municipal Vice Mayor, expedite the revision and updating of the
Local Revenue Code to be able to adjust and/or update the taxes and fees rates
pursuant to Section 191 of R.A. No. 7160 to meet the financial needs of the LGU.

Mayor commented that they had already facilitated the revision of their Local
Revenue Code and already on its first reading.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Sanggunian Bayan expedite the updating and amendment of the Municipality’s Local Revenue Code to increase the taxes and other fees pursuant to sections 129 and 191 of RA 7160 to have additional revenue sources to fund its various  projects.

We recommended that the BAC Chair instruct the BAC Secretariat to post all Invitations to Bid or Requests for Quotation, Notices of Award and Notices to Proceed with the approved contracts in the PhilGEPS, in conspicuous places in the Municipality’s premises and agency website, if available, within the prescribed period in compliance with the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184. In addition, proofs of these posting such as the Certification from the BAC Secretariat and the printout copies from PhilGEPS should be included as supporting documents in the disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that the concerned Local Chief Executive ensure optimum utilization of the 20% of the Annual Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) and fast-track development projects to provide immediate benefits to the constituents and help achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes as required under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the responsible officers, the LDRRM Officer and the Municipal Engineer, to fast-track the implementation of the programs/projects/activities under the LDRRMF to ensure that disaster-prevention, mitigation and preparedness are achieved in the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipality of Alcantara complete the composition of the LSB by having the SK Representative in the SB as member in compliance with Section 98(b)(3) of Republic Act No. 7160 to promote the involvement of the youth sector in the LSB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor reorganize the SWM Committee to comply with the required composition under Section 12 of R.A. No. 9003 and to ensure the performance of their duties and responsibilities especially in monitoring the implementation of the programs and projects. We further recommended that the Focal Person should expedite the completion and submission of the annual progress report to NSWMC to prevent revocation of the Municipality’s 10-year plan.

We recommended that management use a portion of the GAD budget for payment of professional fees, honoraria and other services for gender experts or  specialists engaged to strengthen GFPS capability. In addition, update and enhance their skills through continuing gender education customized according to their functions to fully equip the GFPS in the formulation of their annual GPBs within the context of their mandates and GAD processes to ensure gender mainstreaming in their policies, programs and projects.

We reiterated our recommendation that management strictly observe the provisions of Local Budget Circular No. 103.  The officer responsible in the computation of monthly RATA should always be guided by the daily time records, application for leave of the department heads and assistant department heads and attendance sheet of SB Members during sessions in order to come up with an accurate computation of RATA due.

We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant take extra care in recording of transactions in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

Observe the accounting procedures in the receipt of funds directly credited to the account of the municipality such as receipt of the share from internal revenue collections as provided in the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGU), Volume I. On the part of the Accounting Office, notwithstanding the lapses incurred by the Treasurer’s Office, always ensure that transactions are properly recorded and supported with valid documents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to:

strengthen internal control over the evaluation of the bids and post-qualification of bidders to ensure timeliness, completeness and veracity of the post-qualified documents as reported in the Bid and Post- Qualification Evaluation Reports;

the Municipal Engineer see to it that the reports or documents generated from their office are accurate and be guided by the provision of Section 10 of Annex E of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in the grant of time extension; and

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to adhere to the previous year’s recommendation to account for the unreconciled items appearing on LGU books to faithfully present the correct balances of the account and continue disclosing the unreconciled amount on the Notes to financial Statements until the account or amount is rectified. Give an update on the bank statements she requested from the PNB Head Office as mentioned in the previous year’s Annual Audit Report.
observations.reason: Same observations were noted in the CY 2016 transactions of the municipality.

observations.recommendation: We continued to reiterate our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Budget Officer and the Local Finance Committee to strictly comply with the general limitations on personal services cited under Section 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and Section 6.3.6 of LBC No. 98 in the preparation of the Annual and Supplemental Budgets.  The Municipal Mayor being the Local Chief Executive should always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements were completely complied with.  He should allow only the payment of personal services costs within the personal services limitations.
observations.reason: Analysis of the budgets for CY 2016 disclosed that the municipality again exceeded the PS per budget by P4,423,700.34.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer strictly comply with the PS limitation prescribed under Section 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 in the preparation of the annual budget.   Moreover, the concerned officials should refrain from providing additional PS expenditures until such time that the PS cap has been observed.  Require the concerned municipal officials and employees to refund the personal services/benefits granted to them in excess of the pegged PS limitation.

The municipality should also make efforts in raising its revenues/receipts such as increasing tax collection efficiency and generating revenue related projects/activities to increase the base of PS limitation.
observations.reason: The municipality relied on the budget review of the Sangguniang Panlalawigan that the municipality is not PS Excess, thus actual PS excess of P3,706,227.30 was incurred during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical inventory of all the agency’s inventory items and fixed assets at year-end to determine the validity and reliability of existence of its inventories and properties and submit the corresponding physical inventory reports. He should also demand coordinated efforts among employees to be able to update the submission of reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
require the Municipal Accountant and Inventory Committee to reconcile the differences noted between the balances per accounting records and physical inventory reports;
instruct the Accounting Unit and Supply and Property Unit to update regularly the PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards to ensure the correctness and reliability of both records and to promptly determine any discrepancies or possible losses for appropriate action; and
require the Municipal Accountant and Inventory Committee to investigate the inconsistencies in the classification of PPEs and prepare the necessary adjustments.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee and other concerned offices exert extra effort to pinpoint or identify the persons accountable for the lost properties and require them to request for relief from accountability. Otherwise, hold them liable for the amount equivalent to the net book value of the lost properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to determine the amount attributable only to the unserviceable properties to properly present them in the report. In addition, direct the Inventory Committee to report all the unserviceable properties in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property supported with proper documentation to facilitate its disposal in accordance with COA rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer responsible in the preparation of the Statement of Receipts which form part of the Annual Budget of the municipality and which serves as the basis of the Provincial Local Finance Committee in the review of its budget ensure that the data provided therein are accurate. Avoid including data that will misled the users of the statement.
We also reiterated that management strictly observe the general limitations on personal services cited under Section 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98 dated October 14, 2011.
The municipality should exert efforts in raising its revenues/receipts such as increasing tax collection efficiency and generating revenue related projects/activities to increase the base of PS limitation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contract of Agreement and its attachments within five (5) working days from the execution of the contract as required under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 23, 2009. Instruct the BAC to observe strictly the provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and the new provisions of 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring.
We further recommended that the BAC submit the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and address the deficiencies enumerated.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, should see to it that supporting documents attached to the vouchers are accurate and complete before processing the claim.
We recommended that management be guided with the provisions of Section 6.2 of Annex E of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 on the release of Retention Money.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the LDRRMO to prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP to the Audit Team in accordance with Item 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
We recommended that management refrain from incurring and charging expenses not related to disaster risk reduction management activities against the DRRM fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be guided with the requirement of Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for the complete documentation of every claim against government funds and the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 in recording the transactions of the municipality.

We recommended that management fast track the processing of various requirements for the operation of a sanitary landfill in accordance with Sections 40 and 41 of R.A. No. 9003 the soonest possible time to avert adverse environmental consequences.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
adhere to the previous year’s audit recommendation and account for the unreconciled items appearing in the books of the LGU in order present the correct balances of the account and continue disclosing the unreconciled amount on the Notes to financial Statements until the account or amount is rectified;
give an update on the bank statements requested from the PNB Head Office as mentioned in the previous year’s Annual Audit Report; and
restore the amount of stale checks to cash and recognize the appropriate liability account at the end of the year.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant exert all efforts to verify the nature of the dormant receivables and determine their existence and validity, otherwise, apply for write off in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed by COA Circular No. 2016-005.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that:
the Municipal Treasurer ensure correctness of figures presented on the face of the RCD and refrain from making erasures; and
the Municipal Accountant ensure the accuracy of the amounts of recorded transactions and adjust the balance of the accounts affected by the miscalculated collections.

We recommended that management strictly observe the provisions of Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 103. The officer responsible in the computation of monthly RATA should always be guided by the daily time records, application for leave of the department heads and assistant department heads and attendance sheet of SB Members during sessions in order to come up with an accurate computation of RATA due.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant be guided by COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 so as not to mislead the readers of the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor exercise his power to take legal actions against erring officials and employees; issue demand letter to the Municipal Treasurer to fully settle his cash accountability based on accounting records and those other accountable officers for unremitted collections.  Although a total of P31,607.51 were collected from various collecting officers during the year through payroll deductions, full settlement should still be required from them, otherwise, initiate the filing of appropriate charges to recover the funds of the Municipal Government as mandated in the aforecited provisions of R.A. No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record all funds received for specific projects/purpose in the Trust Fund books and maintain Subsidiary Ledgers to facilitate monitoring of fund transfers. The concerned officials should also submit copies of the MOA/Trust Agreement and other documents that would validate the transfer of the amount from different agencies.

We recommend that the Municipal Engineer make immediate
correction of the deficiencies enumerated above and submit to the Auditor
the corrected accomplishment report for re-inspection.

We recommend for the submission of the distributions lists duly
acknowledged by the persons in-charge of the nine (9) day care centers; Daily
Menus duly approved/recommended by the DSWD; Certification of Day
Care Center teachers that the menu prepared were actually served to day
care students. Notices of Suspension dated January 13, 18 and 27, 2017 were
already issued in this regard.

We recommend that Management submit additional payrolls
equivalent to P4,560.00 have the payrolls certified by the Disbursing Officer
and the Municipal Accountant and with explanation why disbursement
vouchers were used instead of liquidation vouchers. A Notice of Suspension
(NS No. 2017-02) dated January 13, 2017 was issued in this regard.

We recommend Management to ascertain that control procedures on
complete documentation in the distribution of Milk to the beneficiaries are
properly implemented by directing the responsible officials and personnel to:

a. Require the submission of the aforementioned
lacking documents and ensure that the persons who will receive the stocks of
milk to legibly print their name, signature and the date of receipt in the
distribution list.

b. Maintain detailed and complete records supporting the milk
releases and make the documents readily available for verification and
examination to facilitate the timely and substantiated reporting of milk
distribution.

A Notice of Suspension (ND No. 2017-03) dated January 18, 2017 was
issued in this regard.

We recommend management to ascertain that control procedures on
complete documentation in the distribution of fish cages and pens to the
beneficiaries are properly implemented by directing the responsible officials
and personnel to:

a. Require the submission of the aforementioned
lacking documents and ensure that the persons who will receive the fish cages
and pens to legibly print their name, signature and the date of receipt in
the distribution list.

b. Maintain detailed and complete records supporting the fish cage
and pen releases and make the documents readily available for
verification and examination to facilitate the timely and substantiated
reporting of distribution.

A Notice of Suspension (ND No. 2017-07) dated January 31, 2017 was
issued in this regard.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee furnish the Audit Team with the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE duly reconciled with the property records and accounting records pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.  The Municipal Mayor should likewise ensure that physical inventory be undertaken annually and reports be submitted accordingly.

Moreover, we recommended that the responsible officials and employees update the Acknowledgement Receipt of Equipment to pinpoint the accountability, responsibility and liability of accountable officers who have custody of government properties.

It is further recommended that the inventory committee must check the condition of the property, report in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and Waste Materials Report (WMR), the damaged, obsolete and unserviceable property identified in the course of inventory-taking and immediately request for its disposal.  Then, reconcile the inventory report with the accounting records to determine the correctness of property records and any possible losses occasioned by theft or other casualty.
